key_insight: The paper extends compatible-strain mixed finite elements from simplicial meshes to quadrilateral meshes by building shape functions directly in physical space, enabling structure-preserving 2D nonlinear elasticity for both compressible and incompressible solids.
engineering_relevance: This gives solid-mechanics solvers a lower-DOF quadrilateral alternative that can achieve the accuracy of higher-order simplicial compatible-strain elements on practical mesh layouts.
method: Uses Nedelec first-kind shape functions to interpolate the displacement gradient.
Constructs stress shape functions compatible with displacement and gradient discretizations.
Handles incompressibility by element-level pressure condensation, avoiding extra global pressure DOFs.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical examples showing the quadrilateral elements can solve problems that previously required second-order simplicial CSMFEs while using fewer degrees of freedom.
watch_item: The approach relies on numerical integration in physical space because standard Piola-mapped compatible shape functions do not work on arbitrary quadrilaterals, so implementation may be more complex and geometry-sensitive.
paper_title: First-Order Compatible-Strain Mixed Quadrilateral Finite Elements for 2D Nonlinear Elasticity
